93: **Portugal**, 2nd in **Group K** on 5 points, face **Spain**, 1st in **Group H** on 7 points, at AT&T Stadium in Arlington at 12:00 PM PT — with goalkeeper Unai Simón having set a new all-time tournament record of 519 consecutive minutes without conceding, surpassing Walter Zenga's 1990 mark, and Mikel Oyarzabal carrying four goals in the tournament after his brace in the 3-0 win over Austria; **Spain**'s wing options are depleted with Nico Williams (adductor) and Yeremy Pino (shoulder) both expected to miss the match. **Portugal** reached the last 16 via a 2-1 comeback against Croatia — Ronaldo converting from the spot in the 68th minute for the first knockout-stage goal of his six-tournament career, taking his official tally to 976, then Gonçalo Ramos heading in a 94th-minute winner — and Roberto Martínez reports no fresh injury concerns; across 40-plus all-time meetings, **Spain** lead the head-to-head with 18 wins to **Portugal**'s 7, though **Portugal** beat **Spain** on penalties in last summer's Nations League final, the pair's most recent competitive meeting. The winner advances to the quarter-finals on July 11 in Los Angeles to face **USA** or **Belgium**.
94: **USA**, 1st in **Group D** on 6 points, host **Belgium**, level on 5 points with one other team in their group (position not yet separated), at Seattle Stadium at 5:00 PM PT — a venue where **Belgium** have already played three of their five tournament matches, including their 3-2 extra-time comeback win over Senegal. Folarin Balogun, who scored the tournament's leading three goals for the co-hosts, is suspended after a VAR-reviewed red card in the 64th minute against Bosnia and Herzegovina made him only the third player to score in a tournament knockout match and be sent off, following Ronaldinho in 2002 and Zidane in 2006; Ricardo Pepi is expected to start in his place, while Mark McKenzie (foot) and Cristian Roldan (muscle) are doubts. **Belgium** captain Youri Tielemans scored the latest goal in tournament history — a 124th-minute-44-second penalty — to complete a 3-2 comeback from 2-0 down against Senegal, with Leandro Trossard leading all players in the tournament with 16 chances created; Zeno Debast (leg) remains absent for the full tournament, and **USA** have not beaten **Belgium** since the inaugural 1930 edition, losing the last six meetings including a 5-2 friendly defeat in March 2026. The winner faces **Portugal** or **Spain** in the quarter-finals on July 11 in Los Angeles.
91: **Brazil**, level on 7 points with one other team from their group (position not yet separated, GD +6), face **Norway**, 2nd in **Group I** on 6 points, at MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford at 1:00 PM PT — a venue that will host just one further match, the final on July 19. Ancelotti arrives without Raphinha (thigh, sustained vs Haiti) and Lucas Paquetá (thigh/hamstring, forced off at half-time vs Japan), while Casemiro also walked off late in that win, leaving Bruno Guimarães — who leads the tournament with four assists, more than any **Brazil** player at a single edition since Pelé's six in 1970 — as the fulcrum of a reshaped midfield. **Norway**, whose 2-1 win over Ivory Coast via Haaland's 86th-minute tap-in was their first-ever knockout-stage victory at the finals, have never lost to **Brazil** across four meetings (W2, D2), including Kjetil Rekdal's winning penalty in Marseille in 1998; Haaland has scored in each of his last 13 competitive internationals and needs one more goal to become only the eighth European to score in his first four tournament appearances, with Julian Ryerson (thigh, off after 13 minutes vs Senegal) still a doubt at right-back. The winner meets the **Mexico**–**England** victor in the quarter-finals.
92: **Mexico**, 1st in **Group A** on 9 points and the only remaining side not to have conceded a single goal across five tournament matches, host **England**, 1st in **Group L** on 7 points, at the Estadio Azteca at 5:00 PM PT — a venue sitting 2,240 metres above sea level where **Mexico** are unbeaten in 10 all-time tournament matches (W8, D2), a record no other nation holds at a single stadium across three host editions. **Mexico** beat Ecuador 2-0 in the Round of 32 — Julián Quiñones scoring his third goal of the tournament before teeing up Raúl Jiménez — while 17-year-old Gilberto Mora, potentially the second-youngest player to start a knockout match in tournament history (20 days older than Pelé in 1958), came through unscathed; **England**'s 2-1 comeback win over DR Congo was sealed by Harry Kane's 75th-minute equaliser and winner, taking him to 13 all-time tournament goals and past Pelé's career total of 12, though Reece James (hamstring) and Jarell Quansah (ankle) remain out, leaving the right-back position unresolved. The only competitive meeting between these sides ended **England** 2-0 **Mexico** in the 1966 group stage at Wembley; the winner advances to the quarter-finals to face **Brazil** or **Norway**.
